what: 3.6.1. Apply a buffer to a shape
title: Package overview
text: positive buffer expands shape specified number kilometers; negative buffer shrinks . Units always km user. CRS left default (crs=“EPSG:4326”), function used package (st_buffer) automatically transforms kilometers degrees buffer circular 3D surface (buffer might thus appear non-circular plotted 2D). However, recommended always specify projected CRS using buffers avoid potential distortions. Negative buffer example: buffer global scale (shape data.frame country defined) triggers error (shown ):

title: Package overview
text: single layer downloaded maintains original extent. additionally projected desired Coordinate Reference System (CRS). default CRS envar Geographic Reference System (GRS) WGS 84 (EPSG: 4326). commonly used GRS global analyses. GRS assume Earth ellipsoid (spheroid) provide coordinates based 3D surface degrees latitude-longitude values. However, systems ideal compute distances meaning degrees changes based location earth (e.g., 1 degree longitude corresponds ~ 111 km equator approaches zero towards poles). Additionally, users might want improve spatial analysis accuracy, visualize data specific projection, align results data use different CRS. Thus, Projected Reference System (PRS) can used, coordinates based location 2D system. conversion GRS PRS always implies certain amount distortion, thus different PRS optimized different areas globe locally reduce distortion moving 3D 2D representation. Thus, users can use argument “crs” par_set() function specify desired CRS. show example download single variable single source, case 1 km land cover based ESA (Lo Parrino et al. 2025), projection local equal-area projection (EPSG:3035). Inside crs argument, number character representing code PRS must provided. nothing specified, 4326 assumed. crs can also specified prefix ‘ESRI’ ‘EPSG’ according system, e.g. “EPSG:4326” “ESRI:54009”.

text: First, envar define template raster ~ 1 km resolution covering buffer 10 km around occurrence points. buffer covers areas reasonably fall within geographical range species, defined studies developed distribution models butterflies across globe (Gross et al. 2025). layer can thus used template creation kernel density representing intensity sampling effort, via spatialEco package (Evans & Ram 2021). Random background points (10,000) defined area, probability proportional sampling effort defined previous step, using dismo package (Hijmans et al. 2017).
code: # create a layer of sampling effort, as we need a template raster occ_sf <- st_as_sf(Apollo, coords = c("X", "Y"), crs=4326) occ_sf <- st_transform(occ_sf, 3035)  # use 'envar' to create a template raster with a 100 km buffer template <- par_set(shape = occ_sf, buffer = 100, crs = 3035) %>%              melc(vars = "ice")  # create sampling bias raster dens_ras <- spatialEco::sp.kde(x = occ_sf,                                 ref = template,                                 standardize = TRUE,                                 mask = TRUE,                                 res = res(template))  # pick background points proportionally to the sampling effort bg_points <- as.data.frame(dismo::randomPoints(raster::raster(dens_ras), 10000, prob = TRUE))  colnames(bg_points) <- c('X', 'Y') plot(dens_ras) head(bg_points)

title: Species distribution modelling
text: , extract predictor values presence background points, set variables might ecologically-plausible effect species (Nakonieczny et al. 2007), using envar package including automatic check correlation among variables. included four climatic dimensions (annual mean temperature, temperature seasonality, annual total precipitation, precipitation seasonality) 1981-2010 period CHELSA (Karger et al. 2017); three land cover variables (percentage cover meadows, trees, water) measure landscape diversity (Shannon’s index) (Lo Parrino et al. 2025); two topographical variables (slope, northness) (Amatulli et al. 2018). can check Variance Inflation Factor (VIF): Pearson pairwise correlation coefficients: Slope annual mean temperature (bio1) negatively correlated Pearson’s correlations ≥ |0.6|. variables VIF higher 3. Thus, remove one variables; one lower direct impact species physiology distribution (slope).
code: # divide data in spatial blocks for model calibration occ_points <- cbind(st_drop_geometry(occ_sf), st_coordinates(occ_sf)) block <- ENMeval::get.block(occ_points, bg_points, orientation = "lat_lon")  # use 'envar' to extract predictor values at calibration points and check correlations  occ_points$pa <- rep(1, nrow(occ_points));bg_points$pa <- rep(0, nrow(bg_points));data<-rbind(occ_points,bg_points)  # extract predictor values and check for correlation with 'envar' predictors <- par_set(pointsdf = data[,c("X", "Y")], crs=3035) %>%                chelsa(vars = c("bio1", "bio4", "bio12", "bio15"), years =         "1981-2010") %>%                melc(vars = c("meadow", "tree", "water", "shannon")) %>%                topography(vars = c("slope", "northness")) %>%                corr_check() # View the Variance Inflation Factor values print(predictors$vif) # remove slope to avoid correlation issues predictors$data <- predictors$data[, !names(predictors$data) %in% c("slope")]  data <- cbind(predictors$data, data$pa); colnames(data)[colnames(data)=="data$pa"] <- "pa"

title: Species distribution modelling
text: , fine-tune hyperparameters Maxent models (Phillips et al. 2006), using ENMeval R package four-fold spatial block cross-validation (Muscarella et al. 2014). use Maxent widely used algorithm SDMs hyperparameters can easily fine-tuned (Radosavljevic & Anderson 2014). particular, tune regularization multiplier (parameter controls overfitting), combination features (.e., transformations predictors). select combination hyperparameters minimizes difference AUC (Area receiver-operating characteristic Curve, threshold-independent metric predictive performance (Fielding & Bell 1997)) test training calibration sets. higher value imply greater overfitting, predictive ability higher train compared test set (Radosavljevic & Anderson 2014).
code: # Tune maxent models sdms <- ENMeval::ENMevaluate(   occs = data[data$pa == "1", c(2:(ncol(data)-1))],   bg = data[data$pa == "0", c(2:(ncol(data)-1))],   RMvalues = c(1:8),   fc = c("L", "LQ", "LQH"),   partitions = "user",   user.grp = block,   algorithm = "maxent.jar")  # row number of the best model (best test Boyce index) index <- which.max(sdms@results$cbi.val.avg) sdm_best <- sdms@models[[index]] # show tuning table ordered <- sdms@results[order(sdms@results$cbi.val.avg), ]  # best model metrics print(ordered[1, c("rm", "fc", "cbi.val.avg", "cbi.val.sd", "auc.val.avg", "auc.val.sd", "auc.diff.avg", "auc.diff.sd")])

title: Species distribution modelling
text: tuned model good predictive performance limited overfitting. model can thus applied obtain map current habitat suitability Parnassius apollo European Alps, retrieving predictors area using envar R package . added optional function “extr_check”, checked environmental conditions found prediction area consistent provided models training phase. checked “strict” extrapolation (.e. least one predictor outside range found calibration) (Zurell et al. 2012). can plot prediction habitat suitability Parnassius apollo European Alps:
code: # use 'envar' to define predictors over the prediction area (European Alps) predictorsAlps <- par_set(shape = Alps, crs = 3035) %>%                    chelsa(vars = c("bio1", "bio4", "bio12", "bio15"), years = "1981-2010") %>%                    melc(vars = c("tree", "meadow", "water", "shannon")) %>%                    topography(vars = c("northness")) %>%                    extr_check(calib_points = data[,c("X", "Y")], calib_crs = 3035, type = "strict")   # predict with the best model over the European Alps predictionAlps <- dismo::predict(sdm_best, predictorsAlps$data) extr = predictorsAlps$extrapolation$strict plot(extr)

Available variables (23 total) Bioclimatic variables: 1 - SBIO1 - annual mean temperature, annual mean, amt 2 - SBIO2 - mean diurnal range, mean diurnal, mdr 3 - SBIO3 - isothermality, iso 4 - SBIO4 - temperature seasonality, seasonality, temp seasonality 5 - SBIO5 - max temperature warmest month, max temp, warmest month 6 - SBIO6 - min temperature coldest month, min temp, coldest month 7 - SBIO7 - temperature annual range, annual range, tar 8 - SBIO8 - mean temperature wettest quarter, wettest quarter 9 - SBIO9 - mean temperature driest quarter, driest quarter 10 - SBIO10 - mean temperature warmest quarter, warmest quarter 11 - SBIO11 - mean temperature coldest quarter, coldest quarter Monthly mean soil temperatures: 12 - soilT01 - january mean, january, jan 13 - soilT02 - february mean, february, feb 14 - soilT03 - march mean, march, mar 15 - soilT04 - april mean, april, apr 16 - soilT05 - may mean, may 17 - soilT06 - june mean, june, jun 18 - soilT07 - july mean, july, jul 19 - soilT08 - august mean, august, aug 20 - soilT09 - september mean, september, sep 21 - soilT10 - october mean, october, oct 22 - soilT11 - november mean, november, nov 23 - soilT12 - december mean, december, dec depth argument defines soil depth range cm. Options “0-5” (default) “5-15”.
code: processed <- par_set(shape = Alps, crs = 3035) %>%    soilclimate(vars = c("SBIO1", "SBIO10"), depth = "5-15") plot(processed[[1]])

title: Variables
text: function downloads, processes, extracts variables EarthEnv Topography dataset (Amatulli et al. 2018). dataset provides global, cross-scale topographic variables suitable biodiversity ecosystem modeling. Available variables (11 total) 1 - elevation - dem, height, alt, altitude 2 - slope - slope 3 - aspect - aspect 4 - roughness - rough 5 - tri - terrain ruggedness index, ruggedness 6 - tpi - topographic position index, position 7 - vrm - vector ruggedness measure 8 - pcurv - profile curvature, profile curve 9 - tcurv - tangential curvature, tangential curve 10 - eastness - east 11 - northness - north Additional parameters: algorithm - Aggregation method/algorithm use. Options: “md” (median, default), “mn” (mean), “min”, “max”, “sd”. topo_source - Data source. Options: “GMTED” (default) “SRTM”.
code: processed <- par_set(shape = Alps, crs = 3035) %>%    topography(vars = c("elevation", "slope")) plot(processed[[1]])

title: Variables
text: function downloads, processes, extracts variables near-global freshwater-specific environmental variables dataset (Domisch et al. 2015). variables available 1 km resolution capture upstream catchment characteristics, including topography, land cover, soil, climate. Available variables freshwater() function provides access 324 near-global 1-km resolution layers. can filter specific layers within collections using month argument (climatic variables) algorithm argument (topographic, soil, aggregation methods). Temperature (monthly) Use month argument (1–12) select specific months. 1 - monthly_tmin_average.nc – monthly minimum temperature average, min temp average, tmin avg, tmin 2 - monthly_tmax_average.nc – monthly maximum temperature average, max temp average, tmax avg, tmax 3 - monthly_tmin_weighted_average.nc – monthly minimum temperature weighted, min temp weighted, tmin weighted 4 - monthly_tmax_weighted_average.nc – monthly maximum temperature weighted, max temp weighted, tmax weighted Precipitation 5 - monthly_prec_sum.nc – monthly upstream precipitation sum, precipitation sum, precip sum, prec 6 - monthly_prec_weighted_sum.nc – monthly upstream precipitation weighted, precipitation weighted, precip weighted Hydroclimatic 7 - hydroclim_average+sum.nc – hydroclimatic variables average, hydroclim average, hydroclim 8 - hydroclim_weighted_average+sum.nc – hydroclimatic variables weighted, hydroclim weighted Topography 9 - elevation.nc – upstream elevation, elevation, dem 10 - slope.nc – upstream slope, slope 11 - flow_acc.nc – stream length, flow accumulation, flow Land cover 12 - landcover_minimum.nc – upstream landcover minimum, landcover min 13 - landcover_maximum.nc – upstream landcover maximum, landcover max 14 - landcover_range.nc – upstream landcover range, landcover range 15 - landcover_average.nc – upstream landcover average, landcover avg, landcover 16 - landcover_weighted_average.nc – upstream landcover weighted, landcover weighted Geology & soil 17 - geology_weighted_sum.nc – upstream geology, geology weighted, geology 18 - soil_minimum.nc – upstream soil minimum, soil min 19 - soil_maximum.nc – upstream soil maximum, soil max 20 - soil_range.nc – upstream soil range, soil range 21 - soil_average.nc – upstream soil average, soil avg, soil 22 - soil_weighted_average.nc – upstream soil weighted, soil weighted Quality control 23 - quality_control.nc – quality control, qc Using month argument month argument used monthly variables (tmin, tmax, prec). Supplying one several months filters dataset corresponding monthly bands. Using algorithm argument algorithm argument filters specific statistical bands topography, flow accumulation, soils, land cover. elevation slope layers, algorithms map band order: * min – Band 1 * max – Band 2 * range – Band 3 * avg / mn – Band 4 Flow accumulation: * length – Band 1 (stream length) * acc – Band 2 (catchment accumulation) land cover soil variables, algorithm matches text patterns filenames (e.g., “maximum”, “weighted”, “average”). multiple variables requested, algorithm applies entire request, multiple calls may required different algorithms needed.
code: processed <- par_set(shape = Alps, crs = 3035) %>%  freshwater(vars = c("elevation", "slope", "tmin"), algorithm = "mn", month=12) plot(processed[[3]])

title: Variables
text: function downloads, processes, extracts variables Bio-ORACLE v3.0 dataset (Assis et al. 2024). Available variables (24 total) 1 - thetao - Ocean temperature [ºC] (temperature, temp, sea temperature) 2 - - Salinity [-] (sal, salt, saltiness) 3 - sws - Sea water velocity [m.s-1] (velocity, current speed, speed) 4 - swd - Sea water direction [degree] (direction, current direction) 5 - no3 - Nitrate [mmol.m-3] (nitrate) 6 - po4 - Phosphate [mmol.m-3] (phosphate) 7 - si - Silicate [mmol.m-3] (silicate, silicon) 8 - o2 - Dissolved molecular oxygen [mmol.m-3] (oxygen, dissolved oxygen) 9 - dfe - Iron [mmol.m-3] (iron, fe) 10 - phyc - Primary productivity [mmol.m-3] (productivity, pp, primary production) 11 - ph - pH [-] (acidity) 12 - chl - Chlorophyll [mg.m-3] (chlorophyll, chla) 13 - sithick - Sea ice thickness [m] (ice thickness) 14 - siconc - Sea ice cover [Fraction] (ice cover, sea ice) 15 - clt - Cloud cover [%] (cloud, clouds) 16 - mlotst - Mixed layer depth [m] (mld, mixed layer) 17 - tas - Air temperature [ºC] (air temperature, air temp) 18 - par - Photosynthetically Available Radiation [E.m-2.day-1] (light, radiation) 19 - kdpar - Diffuse attenuation [m-1] (attenuation, turbidity) 20 - bathymetry - Bathymetry [m] (depth, elevation, altitude) 21 - slope - Topographic slope [-] (topographic slope) 22 - aspect - Topographic aspect [-] (topographic aspect) 23 - tpi - Topographic position index [-] (topographic position index) 24 - tri - Terrain ruggedness index [-] (terrain ruggedness index, ruggedness) Additional parameters: realm - One “surface” (default), “benthic_minimum”, “benthic_average”, “benthic_maximum” years - Decade range (e.g., “2000-2010” “2090-2100”) ssp - Shared Socioeconomic Pathway future projections (119, 126, 245, 370, 460, 585) algorithm - Statistic apply (max, mean, min, ltmax, ltmin, range). Default “mean”
code: processed <- par_set(marine_ecoregion = "East African Coral Coast", res = 10) %>%    biooracle(vars = "o2", realm = "surface", years = "2000-2010") plot(processed[[1]])

title: Variables
text: function downloads, processes, extracts variables global gridded GDP projections compatible five Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs) period 1850–2100 (Wang & Sun 2022). Available variables (1 total) Economic Metrics: 1 - gdp - gross domestic product, future gdp, economic projection, economy, ssp gdp Scenarios (SSP): ssp argument accepts integers 1 5, corresponding : SSP1: Sustainability SSP2: Middle Road SSP3: Regional Rivalry SSP4: Inequality SSP5: Fossil-fueled Development Years available: Decadal intervals 1850 2100 (e.g., 2020, 2030, 2040…).
code: processed <- par_set(shape = Alps, crs = 3035) %>%    gdpfuture(vars = "gdp", year = 2050, ssp = c(1, 5)) plot(processed)

title: Check for Environmental Extrapolation — extr_check
text: function evaluates whether environmental conditions study area fall outside range conditions observed calibration points. helps identify areas Species Distribution Model (SDM) predictions may unreliable due extrapolation.

title: Check for Environmental Extrapolation — extr_check
code: extr_check(   x,   calib_points,   calib_crs = "EPSG:4326",   type = c("strict", "combinatorial") )

title: Check for Environmental Extrapolation — extr_check
text: x `SpatRaster`, `data.frame`, list containing output previous pipeline steps (e.g., `corr_check()`). calib_points `data.frame` columns `X` `Y` containing coordinates calibration points. locations presence/absence occurrence data collected model training. calib_crs Character numeric. Coordinate Reference System calibration points. Can EPSG code (e.g., `"EPSG:4326"`, `4326`), ESRI code, PROJ4 string, WKT. Default `"EPSG:4326"` (WGS84). type Character vector specifying type(s) extrapolation check. Options `"strict"`, `"combinatorial"`, `c("strict", "combinatorial")` (default). `"strict"`: Uses 1 bin per variable (detects univariate extrapolation). `"combinatorial"`: Uses 5 bins per variable (detects multivariate novelty).

title: Check for Environmental Extrapolation — extr_check
text: `list` containing: elements input already list (e.g., `corr_check()`). `extrapolation`: Either `SpatRaster` layer(s) named "strict" /    "combinatorial" (input raster-based), `data.frame` additional     column(s) named "strict" /"combinatorial" (input point-based).     Values 1 indicate extrapolation (novel environments), values 0 indicate     analog environments.

title: Check for Environmental Extrapolation — extr_check
text: Extrapolation types Extrapolation can occur two ways: Strict extrapolation: least one variable outside range found     calibration data. Combinatorial extrapolation: variable within calibration range,     combination predictors new. function uses environmental overlap mask approach Zurell et al. (2012), implemented `mecofun` package. uses 1 bin per variable strict extrapolation detection 5 bins per variable combinatorial extrapolation detection. Citations: Elith J, Kearney M, Phillips S (2010). "art modelling range-shifting species." Methods Ecology Evolution 1, 330-342. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.2041-210X.2010.00036.x Zurell D, Elith J, Schroeder B (2012). "Predicting new environments: tools visualizing model behaviour impacts mapped distributions." Diversity Distributions 18, 628-634. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1472-4642.2012.00887.x

title: Check for Environmental Extrapolation — extr_check

text: country Character. English name country (e.g., "Italy", "Viet Nam"). Used generate extent shape NULL. continent Character. English name continent (e.g., "Europe", "Africa"). Used generate extent shape country NULL. shape sf object representing study area. can : Polygons: defining region interest. (2013). Uses Realm column CMEC newRealms dataset. mountain_region Character. name mountain region GMBA Mountain Inventory v2.0 (Snethlage et al. 2022). Uses MapName column. mountain_region_cmec Character. name mountain region Center Macroecology, Evolution, Climate definition mountain areas (Rahbek et al. 2019). Uses Name column. glacier_region_19 Character. name glacier region based RGI v6.0 (2017) first-order regions. Uses RGI_CODE column. glacier_region_20 Character. name glacier region based GTN-G 2023 first-order regions. Uses o1region column. freshwater_ecoregion Character Numeric. FEOW_ID freshwater ecoregion Abell et al. (2008). Uses FEOW_ID column FEOW dataset. marine_ecoregion Character. name marine ecoregion Spalding et al. (2007). Uses ECOREGION column MEOW dataset (TYPE == "MEOW"). marine_realm Character. name marine realm Spalding et al. (2007). Uses REALM column MEOW dataset (TYPE == "MEOW"). marine_province Character. name marine province Spalding et al. (2007). Uses PROVINC column MEOW dataset (TYPE == "MEOW"). pelagic_province Character. name pelagic province Spalding et al. (2012). Uses PROVINC column PPOW dataset (TYPE == "PPOW"). pelagic_biome Character. name pelagic biome Spalding et al. (2012). Uses BIOME column PPOW dataset (TYPE == "PPOW"). pelagic_realm Character. name pelagic realm Spalding et al. (2012). Uses REALM column PPOW dataset (TYPE == "PPOW"). pointsdf Data.frame columns X Y representing point coordinates. alpha_hull Logical. TRUE, creates alpha hull polygon around occurrence points using getDynamicAlphaHull function rangeBuilder package (Rabosky et al. 2016) model species distribution ranges. initialAlpha set 2 gradually increased polygon captures least 99% occurrence records. method optimizes balance identifying distinct clusters unique polygons avoiding excessive fragmentation (Roll et al. 2017). Can used conjunction buffer (applied alpha hull creation) land (intersects result land boundary). Default FALSE. References: Rabosky ARD, et al. (2016). BAMMtools: R package analysis evolutionary   dynamics phylogenetic trees. Methods Ecology Evolution 7:701-707. Roll U, et al. (2017). global distribution tetrapods reveals need targeted   reptile conservation. Nature Ecology & Evolution 1:1677-1682. buffer Numeric. buffer distance kilometers expand shrink extent. buffer always specified kilometers regardless target CRS - function automatically converts appropriate units internally (degrees geographic CRS like EPSG:4326, meters projected CRS like EPSG:3035 ESRI:54009). Positive values: Expand area outward distance. Negative values: Shrink area inward distance (useful excluding   coastal/border areas data may different characteristics). points positive buffer: circular buffer radius drawn around   point, effectively converting study area polygons. Default 0. res Numeric. target spatial resolution multiplier. controls cell size output raster stack. Must positive integer (e.g., 1, 5, 10). Default 1 (30 arc-seconds 0.008333333° equator). Higher values multiply original 30 arcsec resolution specified factor. path directory store result download/processing. Default NULL (output stored locally). works corr_check() specified. Specify path including file name extension (e.g. "..//rastername.tif" final export SpatRaster; "..//extracteddataframe.csv" output data.frame). crs Character Numeric. Coordinate Reference System final output. Can EPSG code without prefix (e.g., 4326, 3035, "EPSG:4326"),   ESRI code (e.g., 54009, "ESRI:54009"), PROJ4 string, WKT. NULL, pipeline uses standard default WGS84 (EPSG:4326). specified, downstream environmental layers projected CRS   processing. Note: ESRI codes (53000-54999, 100000+) automatically recognized prefixed   "ESRI:" internally. set_na Logical, default FALSE. TRUE, cell NA least one raster set NA rasters final SpatRaster object. useful output SpatRaster point extraction. scale Character value "small", "medium", "large". represents scale country/continent shapefile retrieved using rnaturalearthdata package. Large implies better definition borders shapefile (scale 1:10). default "medium". useful setting argument country continent. land Logical, default FALSE. TRUE, extent intersected global land boundary Natural Earth (scale defined scale argument). useful clipping marine/pelagic regions land , ensuring buffered areas extend ocean. Note: apply point extractions (pointsdf without buffer). cache Logical, default TRUE. TRUE, source file downloaded downstream functions (e.g. chelsa(), worldclim(), topography()) stored persistent per-user cache directory. download pipeline interrupted (example lost connection) re-launched, resumes stopped, reusing files already retrieved instead downloading . Set FALSE use temporary directory cleared end R session. cache can emptied time clear_cache.

title: Initialize the Environmental Variable Retrieval Pipeline — par_set
text: function download data . Instead, creates standardized spatial template (grid) processes point locations passed downstream functions (like chelsa(), worldclim(), topography(), etc.) ensure retrieved variables perfectly aligned stacked.

title: Initialize the Environmental Variable Retrieval Pipeline — par_set
text: Extent Definition: can define study area using shapefile (sf object),    country name, continent name, various biogeographic boundary types. Resolution: res argument sets target resolution multiplier    base 30 arc-seconds (~1 km equator). Buffering: optional buffer can applied expand study area create    sampling radius around points. buffer always specified kilometers,    regardless target CRS. function automatically converts appropriate    units (degrees geographic CRS, meters projected CRS). Output: input polygon (country/continent), returns list containing      target SpatRaster grid vector mask. input points (without buffer), returns point coordinates extraction. input points buffer, creates polygon geometry around points      returns grid, allowing download raster data area surrounding points.

title: Initialize the Environmental Variable Retrieval Pipeline — par_set
text: Downstream functions align every layer target grid defined using terra::resample()/terra::project(). Continuous layers resampled bilinear interpolation, categorical (factor) layers automatically use nearest-neighbour avoid creating invalid class codes. can force specific method layers , e.g., options(envar.resample_method = "near") (accepted values terra resampling method, "auto" default behaviour described ).

title: Initialize the Environmental Variable Retrieval Pipeline — par_set
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Basic usage with a country italy_grid <- par_set(country = "Italy")  # Download with a shapefile processed_alps <- par_set(shape = "Alps") %>%  melc(vars=c("ice"))  # With a projected CRS and positive buffer (expand by 10 km) italy_buffered <- par_set(country = "Italy", crs = 3035, buffer = 10)  # With a negative buffer (shrink by 10 km to exclude coastal areas) italy_inland <- par_set(country = "Italy", crs = 3035, buffer = -10)  # Points with buffer to create extraction area points_area <- par_set(pointsdf = Apollo, buffer = 10, crs = 4326)  # Using alpha hull to define species range from occurrence points species_range <- par_set(pointsdf = species_occurrences, alpha_hull = TRUE)  # Alpha hull with buffer (buffer applied after alpha hull creation) species_range_buffered <- par_set(pointsdf = species_occurrences, alpha_hull = TRUE, buffer = 50)  # Alpha hull clipped to land boundary species_range_land <- par_set(pointsdf = species_occurrences, alpha_hull = TRUE, land = TRUE)  # Alpha hull with buffer and land intersection species_range_full <- par_set(pointsdf = species_occurrences, alpha_hull = TRUE, buffer = 25, land = TRUE)  # Using zoogeographic regions palearctic <- par_set(zoorealm = "Palearctic")  # Using mountain regions alps_gmba <- par_set(mountain_region = "European Alps")  # Using glacier regions arctic_glaciers <- par_set(glacier_region_20 = "Arctic Canada North")  # Using freshwater ecoregions danube <- par_set(freshwater_ecoregion = 404)  # Using marine ecoregions mediterranean <- par_set(marine_realm = "Temperate Northern Atlantic")  # Using pelagic provinces atlantic_pelagic <- par_set(pelagic_realm = "Atlantic")  # Clip marine realm to land only land_only <- par_set(marine_realm = "Temperate Northern Atlantic", land = TRUE) } # }
